After some debugging, here is what's happening:

1. `reset_db` completely clears the database. This includes all user accounts.
2. The auth logic for `package_create` is special, because `package_create` allows anonymous access [under some circumstances](http://docs.ckan.org/en/2.8/maintaining/configuration.html?highlight=configuration#ckan-auth-anon-create-dataset). As a result, the auth logic for `package_create` only checks whether a user is given in the context, but not whether that user actually exists. Hence, when ckanapi passes along the site user in the context, `package_create` succeeds although that site user doesn't actually exist in the DB anymore.
3. `resource_create` always requires an actually existing user, so this call fails because the site user has been removed from the DB by `reset_db`.
4. When the script is called again, a `ckanapi.LocalCKAN` instance is created before any action functions are called. This in turn calls `LocalCKAN.get_site_username`, which calls CKAN's `get_site_user`, which (undocumentedly) recreates the site user if it doesn't exist. Hence authorization for the site user works again (until `reset_db` is called the next time).

As a workaround, I'm now calling `LocalCKAN.get_site_username` after calling `reset_db`, and this seems to work fine.

I don't think that this is a problem in CKANAPI, hence I will close this issue.

Isn't the scenario you describe really only relevant for Requests if your server is not using HTTPS? If DNS is untrustworthy, then once you've received your Kerberos authentication token your HTTP request may end up in the hands of a malicious actor regardless of how careful we were at the Kerberos stage.

Similarly, if the server **does** use HTTPS then I don't see why it's problematic to send a token generated for malicious.com to trusted.com. Sure, authentication will fail, but that's expected.

So it's not clear to me that being extra strict at the Kerberos stage really buys us anything in this context? Perhaps I misunderstand.

I have no objections to putting this under flag control, but I strongly feel that the default behaviour should be to canonicalise. Reasons:

 1. The default value in krb5 itself is true: https://github.com/krb5/krb5/blob/a4ddc6cf576b4155e6b994307902567f26f752b2/doc/admin/conf_files/krb5_conf.rst
 2. Requests is "HTTP for humans". I think humans will expect Requests to behave like the browsers do by default, which means we should canonicalise. (This is also why we should validate TLS certs by default.)

Right now it is not possible to guarantee that you will get all the results of a query. Eventually we plan to support a gRPC streaming API to return all the entities. Until then, however, we can only return a bounded amount (sometimes it will be less than 300) based on server multitenancy constraints.

Until then, you'll have to use the `more_results` field and the `end_cursor` to implement query batching. Or, you can use one of the `gcloud` client libraries which do the batching for you.

@benley yeah - the recommended approach would be to build a bespoke bdist and then let pex resolve against bdists at build time. most folks create a "wheelhouse" type store that just contains wheels for this purpose and then use `--find-links` against that store. then you'd just populate the wheelhouse via pip or other mechanisms and then let pex simply resolve against the pre-built bdists.

this will not only make your builds generally faster (less compilation work to be done on each build), but also more repeatable and reliable (eliminates an entire class of problems that arise from environmental variance in compilation from dev machine to dev machine).

But in most cases, the task execution will presumably succeed and the vt will be marked valid and the results_dir path never created an additional time. 

In the above case, this bug appears more severe. Since the results_dir has been overwritten from a symlink to a concrete directory, the task will be adding its output to the now concrete `vt.results_dir`. Unfortunately,  the local_artifact_cache and artifact_cache operate using the unique 'current_results_dir`, which is would no longer get the benefit of the task output.

This suggests to me that tasks calling safe_mkdir(results_dir, clean=True) may actually be breaking their ability to cache their work.

The python-pptx project embeds an xlsx file as part of it's chart capabilities, so you could have a look there for some working code. These spots would be a good place to start:

* https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/parts/embeddedpackage.py
* https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/parts/chart.py#L53
* https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/chart/xlsx.py
* https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/chart/data.py#L69

Then you'd need to understand what the XML looks like where the Excel file is actually embedded. opc-diag is handy for that.

Then you'd need to use low-level oxml and lxml calls like OxmlElement() and _Element.append(), etc. to form the right XML in the document XML (document.xml in the .docx package).

You'll need to decide whether to fork and create your own python-docx that includes new part objects and so on, or whether you write "extension" functions (or objects) that you maintain in your own code base, to do the needful as far as adding the Excel-related bits. The latter is generally more flexible as you can  upgrade to new python-docx releases without breaking your code. The former is probably the way if you were going to go for a commit in the main code base, although most folks find it challenging to raise their game to the engineering standard it requires (much of that being the test-driven regimen).

Okay, that makes some sense. I don't really get your use case, but it probably doesn't matter much. I've seen the `mc:AlternateContent` tag show up around vector images. I suspect it's related to older versions of Word using VML for displaying vector images, so a backwards-compatibility measure.

I would recommend the XPath search string: `'./w:p/w:r//w:drawing/wp:inline'`. Note the double-slash moves to just after the `w:r`. That way you're sure you're inside a run. I can't think of any case where you would end up with something you didn't intend using your other string, but might as well be as specific as you can.

Starting from the JPMML-SkLearn library version 1.2.0, the `PickleUtil` utility class will scan all the JAR files in the application classpath for the `META-INF/sklearn2pmml.properties` file, and if found, will automatically register all the listed converter classes with the runtime. For example, here is the list of built-in converter classes: https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/blob/master/src/main/resources/META-INF/sklearn2pmml.properties

You can implement your own `Transformer`, `Selector` and `Estimator` converter classes as appropriate. When done, create a corresponding `META-INF/sklearn2pmml.properties` file, and package everything as a regular JAR file.

During conversion, you can add a list of JAR files to the application classpath using the newly introduced `user_classpath` argument:
```Python
sklearn2pmml(estimator, mapper, "estimator_mapper.pmml", user_classpath = ["/path/to/extensions.jar"])
```

You need to supply Wifi and Cell tower information for the Geolocate API to do it's job. From the [documentation](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/geolocation/intro): The Google Maps Geolocation API returns a location and accuracy radius based on information about cell towers and WiFi nodes that the mobile client can detect.

Ahh - you need to create a 'client' but you can do so by passing an API key. e.g. in the first example on the project README (copied below), you can replace the 'Add Your Key here' string with your API key, e.g. AIzAaSdFAsdFASDf... You can then re-use that client for each request.

```python
import googlemaps
from datetime import datetime

gmaps = googlemaps.Client(key='Add Your Key here')

# Geocoding an address
geocode_result = gmaps.geocode('1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA')

# Look up an address with reverse geocoding
reverse_geocode_result = gmaps.reverse_geocode((40.714224, -73.961452))

# Request directions via public transit
now = datetime.now()
directions_result = gmaps.directions("Sydney Town Hall",
                                     "Parramatta, NSW",
                                     mode="transit",
                                     departure_time=now)
```

Observations: 

  - Not quite sure what to make of the fact that 7 seconds are spent *inside* `HTMLTokenizer.__iter__`. It doesn't seem like it has very much to do, just calling out to the state functions that do most of the work. Maybe it would help to cythonize this?

  - 7 seconds spent inside `HTMLParser.mainLoop` also seems a bit strange. I haven't done a whole lot of digging into that.

  - Another 7 seconds is spent inside `HTMLTokenizer.tagNameState`. Thinking that could also be flattened/cythonized.

  - The tokenizer represents tokens as dictionaries. If a Token class were created, it could be cythonized so that adding tokens to the queue could be done without calling anything.

  - Yet another 7 seconds is spent in `HTMLInputStream.char`. It's pretty damn simple, so a good candidate for cythonization. (if that's a word)

Hey, this would require the IOM access method. That is the only way to connect to SAS running on Windows. Also, to connect remotely to SAS on a Windows machine, SAS will need to be running as a Workspace server, via the Object Spawner. Since you described it as 'a windows server', I'm guessing you are running an Object Spawner and have Workspace servers configured.
If it is really just PC SAS that you run locally, via 'sas.exe', then you cannot connect to that remotely. You can use that SAS kind of an install via IOM, but only with saspy running on the same windows machine.
In sascfg.py, there is a configuration definition named 'iomwin', which would be the appropriate template for your situation; connecting from saspy on Linux to a Windows Workspace server.
Let me know how this works for you and if you have any other questions!
Thanks,
Tom

Hi @treemanzhou,

What version of IOS XR are you using?

From what I can see the flags the router is sending to ExaBGP are these:
{'E': 0, 'L': 0, 'N': 1, 'P': 0, 'R': 0, 'V': 0, 'RSV': 0}

If those are indeed the flags, then I'm afraid it's an IOS-XR bug, unless my interpretation of the draft is incorrect. Quoting the draft:

```
      SID/Index/Label: according to the V and L flags, it contains
      either:

      *  A 4 octet index defining the offset in the SID/Label space
         advertised by this router using the encodings defined in
         Section 3.1.  In this case the V and L flags MUST be unset.

      *  A 3 octet local label where the 20 rightmost bits are used for
         encoding the label value.  In this case the V and L flags MUST
         be set.
```
In this case ExaBGP complains about the index being only 3 octets long, instead of 4, as it should be because both V and L are unset.

Let me know what the BGP dissector in Wireshark is telling you and I'll check if we're parsing the TLV incorrectly if you're not seeing the same flags and prefix length as I mentioned above(look at the UPDATE message right before the NOTIFICATION sent by ExaBGP)

cheers,
Evelio

Hi @ahikaml ,
Thanks for the question! And I'm happy you find this package useful.
To answer your question, when you do frequency domain source separation (or beamforming in some cases), there is an ambiguity whereas you could change arbitrarily the magnitude at every frequency without changing the optimality of your separation filters. One popular way to avoid this ambiguity (the one used in pyroomacoustics) is to *project back* the output from the beamformer onto one of the input channels. The function doing this is [`pyroomacoustics.bss.common.projection_back`](https://pyroomacoustics.readthedocs.io/en/pypi-release/pyroomacoustics.bss.common.html?highlight=bss). In words, this means that the average magnitude and phase delay of the output of the beamformer is changed to matched one of the input channels (the first usually). This means that the reverberation and delay of that microphone will be also matched to the output. Therefore it makes sense to compare the output to this microphone.
Now it is perfectly possible to do the back projection for every input channel (and thus produce a matching number of output channels), and compare all of them with `bss_eval`. This would make sense in the case where the reference microphone chosen only contains a very corrupted version of one of the sources.
If both sources are approximately of equal strength at that microphone, it should be ok to pick only this one for the evaluation.
At least I think! I'd be happy to hear any argument making the case for comparing all the channels. (Note that there is a computational cost to comparing all channels since `bss_eval_images` is not exactly light).

I had the same problem that @RobbieClarken  mentioned above. And I solved it by doing a tiny modification on the line 882 in docker/models/containers.py.(on branch 2.0.2-release)
The original line of codes is
`create_kwargs['volumes'] = [v.split(':')[0] for v in binds]`
And I change it as below:
`create_kwargs['volumes'] = [v.split(':')[1] for v in binds]`

Which I believe might be a bug to fix because the definition of volume parameter for docker engine api is

> An object mapping mount point paths **inside the container** to empty objects.

Hence, the volume parameter should be mapping to `v.split(':')[1]` instead `v.split(':')[0]` because former one means mount point paths inside the container and later one means host path or volume name.

The UFO spec does not say it must start with an on-curve; however even if it does not start with an on-curve, all the UFO apps and libraries that use the Pen protocol like robofab, defcon, etc. *will* nevertheless interpret the first segment of the contour as the first on-curve point in the point list. 
So, for example, if one exports a UFO directly from Glyphs.app and tries to open it in Robofont, then the starting point icon will show up on the following on-curve. The two make different assumptions so in glyphsLib we need to translate between one and the other.

Just throwing in my two cents at this point:

I review and accept pull requests randomly, but I have very little time to actually contribute to Diamond. Let alone keep track of changes and publish proper releases.

I can schedule and run the script that cuts a new release and rely on the changelog being git commits, but that's about all I can offer.

Unfortunately, I'm not a fan of blindly doing that without testing, and that's not an effort that I can commit to at this point.

I get pinging privately about this issue, and it just makes me feel bad that I can't contribute more, but this project has kinda fallen into my lap since @kormoc has been unable to contribute.

I personally really like Diamond, but I'm actively moving Disqus away from it back to collectd just because I can't also be burdened with maintaining our metrics collection library. I have way too much on my plate to `actively` maintain and give this project the love that it needs and deserves.

So like I said, I can help out and do what I can, but doing proper releases are difficult. Need to comfortably maintain backwards compatibility, make sure that things are being versioned accordingly. So cutting new releases means reviewing all of the commits, etc and I'm not comfortable that I can make a good judgement call here.

To me, the biggest problem here is that Diamond keeps all of these crazy collectors in core, and I said this back in https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/issues/181#issuecomment-100332821. If Diamond were split out into core + plugins, it would make this process much much easier.

Then plugins could iterate at their own pace on their own terms without affecting the core Diamond service. Which would make code review and release process much simpler.

As it is today, it's an extremely difficult task reviewing code for collectors that I have 0 context on, for example, collectors for software or hardware that I don't use or interact with. On top of making sure whatever changes are made maintain backwards compatibility.

Again, if we can split out collectors into separate projects (we can use the `python-diamond` namespace), I'd be happy to get more committed to cutting actual releases, and this refactor can be planned for a `5.0` and get back on track.

Without doing this work, I'd be highly uncomfortable in my current position to contribute more at this point.

I hope this sheds some light into my perspective as a maintainer. :cry:

Currently our interface to cluster is very simple: we have 

```
--host  /path/to/file.yml  --to-host /path/to/sync /path/to/also/sync ...
```

and in `file.yml` there are two reserved sections:

1. `DSC` for configuring different `queue`, or machines.
2. `default` for providing a default template of job resource to all modules

Additionally module specific requests can be made.

The proposed change is to still leverage the `file.yml` to have something like:

```
address: localhost
queue_type: pbs
```

indicating that the jobs will be send to `pbs` from `localhost` assuming one is already on the HPC. Then basically instead of syncing `--to-host` I'll just double-check if the folders and files specified already exists. And apparently then we need either another command option `--queue` to specify the queue to use, or just make different copies of the `yml` file and point to them.

As a first pass i'll go for making copies of `yml`. There are already 2 options for DSC on cluster so I guess we better avoid having a 3rd.

A dupe of this issue was recently opened and breathed new life into this topic.

I think, at this point in time, we can safely rule out this issue being addressed.  Development efforts are being concentrated on v2.  v1.x is (mostly) receiving just bug fixes now.  Also, the logger was massively overhauled just recently and with that overhaul came the possibility (which we highly recommend) of storing logs in memory.  I'm going to elaborate on that because it's the detail that nearly renders this issue moot.

The logger component was never _intended_ for long-term archiving of logs.  It's a centralized point of aggregation where developers can query _recent_ application logs via CLI -> controller -> logger.  It's only useful for troubleshooting an issue in real time, and that's all it was ever meant for.

If one wants to aggregate and index logs for the sake of audit, analytics, error detection, etc. there are _many_ existing solutions such as Papertrail, Loggly, and Splunk.  Deis-logger doesn't want to compete with those products-- it would rather just feed logs _to_ them.

This being the case, opting for in-memory storage of logs and "draining" those logs to an external archive is an optimal solution that can be achieved today-- and that strategy is likely to carry forward to v2, where file-based log storage _might_ not even be an option.

Hopefully in-memory log storage + drain strikes operators as a reasonable option.  If the community at large doesn't agree, we can re-open this at a later time.

Something that would be 95% effective (which is really all we need, right?) would be to run `pip.main()` within the exitfunc, using `atexit`. 
I should clarify why I'm comfortable with that, but not with a raw `pip.main()` in the middle of a running CLI command.

All of the dangerous conditions for doing `pip.main()` arise from either doing imports or reads via `pkg_resources` *after* `pip.main()` has run.
I would say that it's highly unlikely that anyone would write code which runs an import of a previously unimported module in a registered `atexit` handler, or which uses `pkg_resources` in an `atexit` handler. Allowing for failure in these obscure and unlikely scenarios seems like an okay tradeoff, so long as we leave ourselves a paper-trail of comments from whence we can deduce that this is at least one way that `globus upgrade` could fail.

I think the best approach is to print a warning message before we start, as in:

```shell
$ globus update
You are currently running the Globus CLI, version x.y.z . An upgrade to p.q.r is available.
Would you like to proceed? [Y/n] 

The Globus CLI will now update itself.
In the event that an error occurs or the update is interrupted, we recommend uninstalling and reinstalling the CLI.

...
```

> There needs to be a way to construct the access list without prior knowledge. So there should be a execution mode that works with an empty access list (or a placeholder) and that keeps track of which storage slots are accessed.

IMO doing this with an execution mode is a bad idea; it would break in any scenario where the keys accessed depend even slightly on variables that could be affected by third parties. The access lists should be generated by something which gets added to Viper; you would pass a function name and arguments and Viper would reply back with what addresses and storage keys could get read or written.

> Good point, my guess is that with stateless clients gas costs of state access in general will need to be revised significantly.

Yep! My personal preference at the moment BTW is that we charge a clean 4 gas per byte for all transaction or witness data, and we compute witness sizes based on the trie/state root at the start of collation execution (ie. part of the witness verification step of verifying a collation).

> I think there would be something like check_PAYGAS_execution which basically acts like execute_transaction but with a gas limit(specified by miner?) and returns True if PAYGASopcode is execute or returnFalse` if gas limit reached.

I think the solution may be simpler. Rather than just returning success or failure, we could return two kinds of failure, one where PAYGAS is executed, and if so with what gasprice, and the other where PAYGAS is not executed.

An FYI for @murduk and anyone else looking to work on this or any other bounty'd issue.  We're only going to consider an issue as formally claimed at the point where a pull request has been opened demonstrating active work on the issue.

This is meant to prevent people from "claiming" something on a Monday with the intent to work on it later in the week or that weekend.  This is **not** meant to discourage people from claiming issues, but rather to incentivize people to only claim things when they have the time to actively work on them.  

We exercise some common sense, granting some leeway to claim something and get a pull request open later that day.  This policy is directed towards a claim on gitcoin that shows no work after a day or two.

We're working on getting some formal procedures in place for bounties which will document these policies.

I feel ok to not be to harsh with commit message conventions. However, I feel like we shouldn't go to extremes with merging "dirty" commits. Like, commits that try out something, then revert it, try something else instead. These things are:

- bloating the commit history
- introduce commits into the history that are known to not pass CI
- make `git bisect` less efficient
- skew statistics
- etc

I think that the PR should either contain clean commits prior to merging or should otherwise be squashed via the Github UI. I know that @carver doesn't like his PRs to be squashed / rebased to preserve "local matchability" but that is fine as long as the PR is clean. Test is likely going to require a bit of discovery work to figure 1-2 things out.  Here is the basic structure.

- Mine a chain of a few blocks (2-3) using an in-memory database.  This is chain `A`
- Make a full copy of the database used in chain `A`, and create a separate chain instance with this db.  This is chain `B`
- Mine 3 more blocks on chain `A`
- Mine 5 more blocks on chain `B`.  Make sure the first block you mine is different from the first block that was just mined on chain `A`.  This can be done by including a transaction in the block.

Now we have what we need to test our API.

- extract the blocks from chain `B` that you mined which are not in chain `A`.
- In chain `A` use the `import_block` API to import the blocks one-by-one.

You should be able to then make the necessary assertions on the return values of the `import_block` calls.

Folks, we had our internal conda meeting today and discussed this PR and the surrounding issues at length. The tl;dr is this: we'd like to enable some _optional_ behavioral changes in `conda` to get past the current impasse, while we work to find the best _longer-term_ solutions to the various issues we're seeing here.

### Feature optimization

@mbargull: is it possible for you to modify this PR so that the original behavior is maintained by default, but your modified priorities are enabled by the use of a command-line option, say `--alt-features` (or `alt_features: true` in the `.condarc`)? I haven't done a code review of this PR myself yet, but I can do so, to make sure there aren't other unintentional changes.

### Build number maximization

We _cannot_ force conda to consider only the latest build numbers currently, by default. For one thing, many user environments, and many _packages_, pin to particular builds of packages. We have to make sure those pins are respected regardless of new builds that may have been issued. It turns out that forcing maximum build numbers when that's possible, while respecting _all_ pinning requirements, is a tough thing to pull off algorithmically (in part because of the second reason below).

But this is not the _only_ reason we can't do it. A limited number of package builders have been using build numbers in an "off-label" manner in order to support the creation of multiple _variants_ of a single package. For example, build number `1` might link to Qt4, and build number `2` might link to Qt5. This is _not_ the right way to do this: build _string_ manipulation should have been used instead; e.g., `qt4_1`, `qt5_1`, much like conda build does by default for Python and NumPy variants. Conda build 3.0's hash string approach has actually made it easier to avoid build number gaming like this---but given the _current_ state of the ecosystem, we can't forbid this just yet.

However, _we propose to make a "latest build only" behavior an optional feature_, off by default for now. That is, we'll have a keyword argument, say `--latest-builds-only` (or `latest_builds_only: true` in `.condarc`); and if this is `true`, `conda` will not allow older build numbers to be installed. This will be made, of course, on a per-package and per-channel basis. I don't have a schedule for this one. If anyone wants to take a crack at it...

### What to do with broken packages?

From @johanneskoester:

> i.e., if something breaks, people fix it and increase the build number.

As I pointed out above, this is usually true, but not always. But let's assume that it _is_ true. It is _still_ necessary to do do something about the old, broken package. _Package maintainers cannot just leave the broken package in place_, because as we have seen, `conda` will still select it, and that simply cannot change any time soon. There are four approaches you can take:

1. _Remove the package altogether_. This is the easiest thing to do, but we can't do this at Anaconda; we need to maintain package history for reproducibility reasons. But other channel maintainers may be free to do this.
2. _Move the package to a "broken" or "archive" subchannel/label_.
3. _Hotfix the metadata in the `repodata.json` file for the broken package_. This what we currently do at Anaconda for the defaults channel. 
4. _Mark the package as "broken" so that `conda` will not consider it anymore_. This is effectively just a simpler version of option 3. For instance, it could be achieved by adding a dependency that is impossible to satisfy.
5. _Unpack the broken tarball, correct its metadata, re-pack, re-upload_. For awhile, we had second thoughts about option 3, so we did a "cleanup" pass. We got quite a bit of pushback on this one, and decided to revert to option 3 henceforth.

What is _not an option_ is doing nothing, leaving the package in place, with no metadata change. It is very important that we communicate to package builders that this is the case. If you encounter a package that is broken, please file an issue with the maintainers encouraging a correction but also the _removal or correction of the broken build_.

Note that Options 3 and 4 are not yet available to community channel maintainers (conda-forge, bioconda, etc.) This is due to the specific design of Anaconda.Org; our desire to make the defaults channel as performant and responsive as possible _also_ allows us to hotfix the metadata in this manner. But we _are_ working on a standardized approach to specifying metadata "corrections" that all channel maintainers can adopt and use.

### Better prevention of incompatible package combinations

We need to come up with better ways to erect algorithmic "barriers" that prevent incompatible packages from being installed in the same environment. This is currently accomplished entirely through the dependency metadata; a package that works only with Python 2, for instance, can add `python 2.*` to its dependency list, and `conda` knows it can never install that in a Python 3 environment. Conditional dependencies and [variant metapackages](https://gist.github.com/mcg1969/20667002c79f702e3c7003c81b5f24c8) can play a part here. Techniques like these are employed, for instance, to ensure every package installed on Windows relies on the same Visual C++ runtime (when relevant), or uses the same BLAS variant for numerical code.

It's clear now there are some other package sets that need to be "separated" from each other; for instance, the conda-forge and Anaconda builds of R packages. We need to actively seek out solutions on a case-by-case basis.

These barriers not only prevent the user-facing unpleasantness of broken environments, but they also help speed up conda's solver too. That's because they make it very easy for conda to throw out entire subsets of the package ecosystem during the initial, faster solver passes.

### Whew!

That was even more of a mouthful than my last post! Thanks for your patience. Let me know if you have questions...

> Is there some way we can get conda to report an error in this case?

Yes.

    conda config --set path_conflict warn

or 

    conda config --set path_conflict prevent

The default in conda 4.3 is

    conda config --set path_conflict clobber

@mcg1969 As I have shown above, `conda create` doesn't seem to take those cached `repodata.json` into account.

> Why does it need them?

The package cache is itself a conda repo.  By definition, a conda repo is a `repodata.json` file along with accompanying tarballs.

> Should it need them?

Perhaps not. But it would take a lot of careful engineering work to get all the behavior to be correct without them.

> Does this mean (as I think I have observed) that conda clean --tarballs will break conda create --clone in a way that should at the very least be handled more elegantly than returning a stack trace to the user? 

Yes, after `conda clean`, the user shouldn't expect to be able to use any "offline" behavior.  However, I think "offline behavior" has now actually been conflated with `--clone`.  We should obviously have more elegant messaging.  But I also think that the deeper issue is that our implementation of `--clone` is incorrect.

@ColinDuquesnoy just to clear this up a little bit: the TP-DEBUGGER is a pre-processor that tranforms cob-files to cob-files while adding debugging code (it was written with GnuCOBOL so you could compile it with the MinGW package and distribute it along). It's "normal" process finishes with a call to cobc but you can only pre-process a file if you want to. It uses curses output (needs a "real" terminal).

@eugeniodilo you can compile the TP-DEBUGGER yourself, place it somewhere and use it via the following hack: go to preferences (F2) -> compiler, replace the compiler path with a batchfile where you call the animator first, then the actual cobc - but only on real compilations.

The batchfile could look like the following (you can remove the calls to VCVARS if you only want to use the shipped GC1.1 MinGW package):

~~~~~cmd
@echo off
setlocal

:: use without "rem" for running the animator
rem set doanim=1

:: check of compiler version
if [%1]==[--version] (
   cobc.exe %*
   set rc=%errorlevel%
   goto end
)

:: store all options in the same vars (for getting the last option & optionpath)
FOR %%O IN (%*) DO CALL :storeopt %%O

:: check for working compiler - handle early
if [%option%]==[%TEMP%\test.cbl] (
   call "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 10.0\VC\bin\VCVARS32.bat" 1>NUL
   cobc.exe %*
   set rc=%errorlevel%
   goto end
)

:: background check
if [%optionpath%]==[%TEMP%] (
   cobc.exe %*
   set rc=%errorlevel%
   goto end
)

:: normal compilation
call "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 10.0\VC\bin\VCVARS32.bat" 1>NUL
if [%doanim%]==[1] (
   animator %option% -c
   cobc.exe %all-prev-options% %optionfile-plain%.ani
   set rc=%errorlevel%
) else (
   cobc.exe %*
   set rc=%errorlevel%
)
goto end

:storeopt
set all-prev-options=%all-options%
set option=%1
set optionpath=%~dp1
set optionfile=%~nx1
set optionfile-plain=%~n1
set all-options=%all-options% %1
goto :eof

:end
echo exit with %rc%
endlocal
~~~~~

In general if the VHDL parser fails the `run.py` file will continue anyway and disregard dependency scanning of that file but print a stack trace. This is because a syntax error could trigger a parser bug and it is better to let the simulator compiler tell the user what is wrong with the file.

Also I should mention that I am not really that enthusiastic on adding more ad-hoc parsing options to the VUnit VHDL parser at it is in conflict with the goals that it should be robust and fast. The VUnit VHDL parser does not need to be a complete standard compliant parser but a good heuristic way to get the dependencies of the source files. 

For general purpose analysis of VHDL code the VUnit parser architecture we have is not the right tool for the job and adding more regex to it in the same manner as now without making it a real parser with tokenization and recursive grammar just makes it more likely to fail on corner cases found out in the wild where there are millions of lines of VHDL code going through the it. 

Also the VUnit VHDL parser is not part of the public API of VUnit and is not really intended to be used by others I would not recommend it but people have been known to do it. If you want real parsing of VHDL it is better to use a project which has those goals and a the right software architecture for it such as; https://github.com/Paebbels/pyVHDLParser made by @Paebbels

It is possible that we choose to integrate a full VHDL parser into VUnit but it will not be based in any way on the architecture of `vhdl_parser.py` and most likely be a separate project from VUnit.

There are two kinds of exception. There's an empty list, which generates the `NoIndices` or `NoSnapshots` exceptions, and everything else. Try using [`ignore_empty_list'](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.0/option_ignore_empty.html) to continue past the exception you hit. 

The reason for two different kinds of exceptions is that an empty list might be a benign, expected condition for new clusters and indices. The other kind of exception is almost always bad.

Okay, so I did some digging, here's what I found.

Real-time system protection had basically no impact, we're talking sub 100 ms if any.

Then I used to `procmon` to try and figure out what it was that was taking time. There's a lot of file scanning going on in the `C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\Lib` directory.

I noticed the occasional ACCESS DENIED error when it was trying to write back a precompiled python script and given that it's installed under Program Files that's normal.

I then ran in an elevated command prompt and the performance doubled.

I then tried running in a normal command prompt and the performance was the same. So there's a 2x perf hit right there because you are not running as an admin all the time!?

But even with a 2x perf gain it's IMAO too slow. About 1.5 seconds for each run, even if it's not doing anything.

I'm assuming you can trace this back to all the file scanning that is still happening. I think you need to have an index file so that you don't have to resort to full file scanning every time you run a command. App-Gateway WAF Config (2017-03-01)
--------------

**Group Help**
```
Group
    az network application-gateway waf-config: Configure the settings of a web application firewall.
        This command is only applicable to application gateways with SKU type of WAF.

Commands:
    list-rule-sets: (PREVIEW) Lookup information on available WAF rule sets, rule groups, and rule
                    IDs.
    set           : Update the firewall configuration of a web application.
    show          : Show the firewall configuration of a web application.
```

**Updated waf-config set**
```
Command
    az network application-gateway waf-config set: Update the firewall configuration of a web
    application.

Arguments
    --enabled   [Required]: Specify whether the application firewall is enabled.  Allowed values:
                            false, true.
    --disabled-rule-groups: Space separated list of rule groups to disable. This disables the entire
                            group. To disable specifc rules, use '--disabled-rule-ids'.  Values
                            from: az application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets.
    --disabled-rules      : Space separated list of rule IDs to disable.  Values from: az
                            application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets.
    --firewall-mode       : Web application firewall mode.  Allowed values: Detection, Prevention.
                            Default: Detection.
    --no-wait             : Do not wait for the long running operation to finish.
    --rule-set-type       : Rule set type.  Default: OWASP.  Values from: az application-gateway
                            waf-config list-rule-sets.
    --rule-set-version    : Rule set version.  Values from: az application-gateway waf-config list-
                            rule-sets.
```

**List-rule-sets Help with Examples**
```
Command
    az network application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets: (PREVIEW) Lookup information on
    available WAF rule sets, rule groups, and rule IDs.

Arguments
    --group    : List rules for the specified rule group. Use '*' to list rules for all groups. Omit
                 to suppress listing individual rules.
    --type     : Rule set type to list. Omit to list all types.
    --version  : Rule set version to list. Omit to list all versions.

Examples
    List available rule groups in OWASP type rule sets.
        az network application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets --type OWASP


    List available rules in the OWASP 3.0 rule set.
        az network application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets --group * --type OWASP --version
        3.0


    List available rules in the 'crs_35_bad_robots' rule group.
        az network application-gateway waf-config list-rule-sets --group crs_35_bad_robots
```

After the collection is created, you can query for the collection's offer which indicates the throughput after that you can replace the existing offer.

The following code shows how to find the relevant collection offer:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-documentdb-python/blob/master/test/crud_tests.py#L253-L257

After that you should replace the new offer as 'offerThroughput' in the retrieved offer and try to replace it.

https://github.com/Azure/azure-documentdb-python/blob/master/test/crud_tests.py#L3615

When replacing you only need to modify 'offerThroughput' of the offer

CMake currently has two ways of finding dependencies via find_package(): the classical one uses a find script, e.g., FindBullet.cmake, that defines variables such as ${BULLET_INCLUDE_DIRS} and ${BULLET_LIBRARIES} that can be used in a project. This is either written by the developer himself or ships with CMake in the Modules directory (the current CMake version includes a FindBullet.cmake). These can then be used in target_link_libraries(foo ${BULLET_LIBRARIES}) and include_directories(${BULLET_INCLUDE_DIRS}) or target_include_directories(foo PUBLIC ${BULLET_INCLUDE_DIRS}). ${BULLET_LIBRARIES} should contain absolute paths to the file and if available debug and optimized (release) configurations. Using link_directories() is not recommended any more (https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.9/command/link_directories.html).

The new approach lets projects ship a config with their installation that can be used by other projects without supplying the find script. Most of them also include targets that enable relocation of installations, as the absolute paths are no longer hard-coded but are stored in the config files. The one Bullet currently provides however does not provide absolute paths for the files, uses link_directories(), and is not relocatable. I just tried and could not compile an external project using this file on Windows with Visual Studio.

If a programmer uses the old config file or the CMake find script with target_link_libraries(foo ${BULLET_LIBRARIES}) instead of target_link_libraries(foo LinearMath BulletCollision BulletDynamics), the new config file would behave identical as it now contains "Bullet::LinearMath Bullet::BulletCollision Bullet::BulletDynamics" (and the other ones specified at the end of CMakeLists.txt). The other variables are not required any more, but can still be used. I set these to absolute paths however for compatibility with other CMake find scripts.

The namespace in combination with "::" is recommended to allow CMake to identify imported targets (see https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.9/manual/cmake-developer.7.html#a-sample-find-module).

GNUInstallDirs takes care of platform-specific directory names according to GNU coding standards, e.g., lib could also be lib64, lib/i386-linux-gnu, or lib/x86_64-linux-gnu.

I think @cecton idea about `Proxy` class, which encapsulates all proxy parameters and alghorithms is a good idea.

Actual implementation of proxy support is not so flexible, some issues are:
-  it's hard to fully control headers sent to proxy even with `proxy_headers` setting, e.g. CONNECT:443 send headers: `Accept: */*` and `Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate`, which are inappropriate in this case. These headers cannot be skipped, because lack of  `proxy_skip_auto_headers`,
- some proxy types may need even more `proxy_` settings, e.g. see discussion  #2630,
- implementation CONNECT:80 proxy type by end user requires inheritance from `TCPConnector`, copying whole `_create_proxy_connection` method and change one or two parameters in its body (and then end user must track all changes in this method in future `aiohttp` releases),
- implementation CONNECT:80 proxy type by `aiohttp` requires passing around more `proxy_` parameters.

With separate `Proxy` class it will be easier address these issues. `Proxy` class can have whole bunch of  `proxy_` parameters without complicating `ClientSession()` or `session.request()` API. Also, one can easily inherit `Proxy` object and modify its behavior for his own needs.


Using `Proxy` class in simple case can be as easy as `URL` from `yarl`. `proxy` parameter can accept `str` or `Proxy` instance:
```
proxy = Proxy(url)
proxy = Proxy(url, http_headers={...}, http_auth=("user", "pass"), ...)
session.get('http://test.com', proxy=proxy)

# or in the simplest case:
session.get('http://test.com', proxy='http://my.proxy.com')
```

`proxy` parameter can be supported by `ClientSession.__init__` and by `ClientSession._request`. It is possible then to override proxy for specific request with other proxy, e.g:
```
async with ClientSession(proxy=proxy1) as session:
    async with session.get(url1) as resp: # proxy1 is used here
        ...
    async with session.get(url2, proxy=proxy2) as resp:
        ...
    async with session.get(url3, proxy=proxy3) as resp:
        ...
```

`Proxy` instance will by passed down to `ClientRequest` instance and then proper methods will be called from `Connector` instance (e.g. `req._proxy.create_connection()`).

I can imagine even `ProxySet` class which holds set of `Proxy` instances. End user can then inherit `ProxySet` and implement his own strategy for using proxies (e.g. different proxy for different protocols or urls, or rotating proxy strategy, etc.). `ProxySet` instance can be provided in `proxy` parameter also for API simplicity.

I believe if you make it match my post above, it should work (e.g. use a partially applied function, `functools.partial`, to set that as the default on the json.dumps call).

```
import json
from functools import partial


def help_serialize_json(obj):
    """Your custom setup here"""


# note the usage of `default` here, which is made to be the kwarg for the built-in json.dumps
custom_dumps = partial(json.dumps, default=help_serialize_json)


class Properties(web.View):
    async def get(self):
        context = {}
        # this uses the full `json.dumps` that has the single kwarg defaulted to handle your custom types
        return json_response(context, dumps=custom_dumps)
```

This is in line with how 'save as' works in most applications: it changes the file path you're working on, so if you open A and then 'save as' B, subsequent saves will write B until you use 'save as' again.

Some applications have a separate 'save a copy' action, which lets you write file B but keep working on A. I don't think that's a priority to add to the notebook - not many other apps have it.

If the browser is talking to a notebook server, I don't think you can stop the user playing with the REST API. They don't need to use the kernel for that - they can open the browser's Javascript console and run code in the webpage.

There are a couple of approaches you could take:

- Run the notebook server in the docker container as well, and let the user do what they like with it. This is what Jupyterhub and Binder do - the user only has access to their own notebook server, so they can't interfere with anyone else.
- Run kernels without a notebook server using something like [kernel_gateway](https://github.com/jupyter/kernel_gateway/), and hook them up to web pages that don't try to load/save .ipynb files, e.g. [Thebe](https://github.com/oreillymedia/thebe) (though it looks like Thebe is unmaintained now, but a similar idea is still possible).

On 27 January 2017 at 03:32, Cory Johns <notifications@github.com> wrote:

[ taking most of this on board ]

> The first approach in the proposal section seems incompatible with actions.
>
> Not at all. It simply acknowledges that actions are fundamentally
> different from Juju life-cycle events but should still be able to inform
> the reactive system via flags in the same way.
>
> Actions have a hook context, so the juju-run tasks kicked off by the
> persistent queue would block until the action exits.
>
> This is merely an optimization to reduce latency and the number of Juju
> contexts that are used. I agree that it may be useful to make that
> optimization, but it may also be that the action might be better served by
> being decoupled from the handlers that it might trigger.
>
Its a deadlock. An action must signal its results before exiting, but the
handlers that generate the result are blocked until the action exits. The
only way I see it working is for the handlers queue to be drained in the
action's hook context, during which at some point one of the handlers can
call action-set or action-fail.

This proposal does not address how to handle charm upgrades. Again, the
> first approach proposed seems to fail here as Juju will be replacing the
> charm under the persistent queues feet. Inflight handlers will become
> invalid (as their locations in the .py files has changed, per @only_once
> problems), or if they are resident in RAM or .pyc then handlers will be
> trying to access invalid resources on disk such as templates.
>
> This is a good point that we will certainly have to keep in mind, but I
> don't think we can avoid it by worrying about which Juju context the
> handlers are processed in. Regardless of whether we try for the
> optimization of depleting the queue within the current Juju context if
> possible, it is entirely possible for flags to be changed entirely outside
> of a Juju context, which could cause handlers to be queued which might not
> be processed until after Juju has updated the charm code. My point with
> that possible optimization is to acknowledge that, while the queue needs to
> exist outside of a Juju context, the worker that processes that queue might
> not.
>
> However, the queue is really just a list of pending handlers. That list
> should be symbolic, and should gracefully handle reactive handlers no
> longer existing, just like Juju gracefully handles if an upgrade removes a
> hook that was already queued. Upgrades would need to cause the handler
> conditions to be re-evaluated regardless to have new handlers be picked up.
>
Its also possible for an upgrade-charm happening at the wrong time to leave
the charm in an unrecoverable state. Consider three handlers, a() triggers
b() and b() triggers c(). Lets say a() runs, queuing b(). Now upgrade-charm
kicks in, changing the charm so a() now triggers c() directly and b() is
gone. The charm is now stuck. You will not be able to reliably run
upgrade-charm unless you can guarantee the system is quiescent, and that is
probably never for Juju aware applications.

So those would be my main reasons why I think the first proposed
implementation will not work (using juju-run to launch each handler
individually).

In my mind, the hook context is a transaction. A main thrust of this
proposals seems to be to make handlers their own transaction (so if a
handler successfully runs, its reactive state changes persist and will not
be rolled back if a subsequent handler fails). This is problematic, and why
I'm now thinking the second proposed implementation will not work either.
If you run handlers a(), b() in the same hook context and b() fails, then
any changes a() made to the hook environment are rolled back. But with this
proposal, a() will not be invoked again and the charm now likely stuck,
waiting for responses to signals it thought were sent but were in fact
rolled back by Juju.


> If nothing else, the rename from reactive "states" to reactive "flags" is
> a breaking change, and I think it affords us a good opportunity to take a
> hard look at the areas where we can make real improvements.
>
In isolation, this could be backwards compatible (leaving the old methods
there as aliases, clearly documented as deprecated). But yes, if there are
backwards incompatible changes in the near future then this change could
wait until then and it would be a little cleaner.

I agree the relation metaphor will be difficult to fix without backwards
incompatible changes (if it can be backwards compatible, great, but I don't
think we should compromise on a lesser model because of it). WIthout
`@hook` we also lose $JUJU_HOOK_NAME, $JUJU_RELATION_NAME,
$JUJU_RELATION_ID and $JUJU_REMOTE_UNIT (any code relying on these will
only function in specific hook environments). I guess this is what makes
actions special, in that like @hook we have code that must run in the
original hook context.

--

Hi Phil,

Sure, happy to try to answer your questions.

> Could you explain the motivation for a distinction in the third party imports between 'local' and remote? 

The motivation is so that those reading the code know what packages are provided by the author's company or organization, and which are provided by the broader community.  In grouping these imports together, as flake8-import-order currently does, this distinction cannot be discerned.  Since the process for changing an internal package is often much easier and attainable than, say, changing the argument order in a function in a popular third-party package, flagging this distinction by visually separating the import blocks is helpful.

> Could you also explain how you see this distinction being applied (or not) to the various styles this projects support?

All the styles accommodate this change if the application-package-names value is set.  The module would simply throw an error when local packages are imported before third-party packages.  In not setting the flag, there is no difference in the linting result.

> Finally I am struggling to see this as being a generic addition that is not specific for your workflow, could you explain why you (presumably) think it isn't?

Many companies use internal PyPi or DevPi repositories to distribute internally-written Python packages for use by their own Python applications in production.  Still others distribute these packages using Yum, Apt, or other OS-level package managers.  Since the code contained in these packages is not a relative import, its import line should not be grouped with the relative imports.  But, as stated earlier, these are not truly third-party imports since the code belongs to the company or organization.  Thus, a new import section is needed.

I don't understand the use case here.  There is already a handle to the object that you can check for this information.  Why does it need to be a method specifically (it's already an `@property` method)?

The reason is that the same can be preferably achieved by transformation of the objective function using `cma.fitness_transformations.ScaleCoordinates`: ```python import cma ScaleCoordinates = cma.fitness_transformations.ScaleCoordinates felli_3d_scaled = ScaleCoordinates(cma.ff.elli, [100, 10, 1]) assert felli_3d_scaled([0.01, 0.1, 1]) == cma.ff.elli([1, 1, 1]) cma.fmin(felli_3d_scaled, [1, 1, 1], 1) cma.plot() ``` In this example the mis-scaling of the ellipsoid function is reduced to 10 (= 1000/100). The disadvantage of rescaling the initial covariance matrix is that there is a numerical limit to the condition number of the covariance matrix. Even if we start with the identity, in my experience we may end up close or beyond these limits in a practical application (like in issue #10). Hence rescaling the objective function is the preferable approach. Still, the module gives also the possibility for an additional scaling in the distribution or for changing the initial covariance matrix. ```python cma.s.pprint(cma.CMAOptions('stds')) {'CMA_stds': 'None # multipliers for sigma0 in each coordinate, not represented in C, makes scaling_of_variables obsolete', 'CMA_teststds': 'None # factors for non-isotropic initial distr. of C, mainly for test purpose, see CMA_stds for production', 'scaling_of_variables': 'None # depreciated, rather use fitness_transformations.ScaleCoordinates instead (or possibly CMA_stds).\n Scale for each variable in that effective_sigma0 = sigma0*scaling. Internally the variables are divided by scaling_of_variables and sigma is unchanged, default is `np.ones(N)`'} ```

And of course, the handling of flags specific to subcommands is the opposite; `letsencrypt --csr file.csr auth` will fail :(.

There are a number of [ugly solutions](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23296695/permit-argparse-global-flags-after-subcommand) to this problem discussed on stack exchange.

Another option, not discussed there is:

- use my hackish preprocessing of the command line to always move the verb to the very end
- move all subcommand arguments into their own argument groups, rather than attaching them to the subcommands

Downsides:

 - no erroring out if a nonsensical subcommand argument is provided when that subcommand isn't running.  Is that actually a problem, I wonder?  It seems we might be overzealous in this sense right now: if `--csr` makes sense for `auth`, does it not make sense for `run`

@abalkin @tim-one I did _not_ say it's not a variant of the pytz kludge. I suspect the approaches are very similar, since in both cases we're trying to store information about the `datetime` object in the `tzinfo`.

What I was saying was that I think this approach is a bit of a lighter touch, because:

1. it uses the parent `fromutc()` for the actual calculations, and only determines the "if it's DST" part based on how much the shift in the nominal timestamps was.
2. `dateutil` timezones are lazily-calculated, and as far as I can tell, `pytz` timezones are eagerly calculated, so you can't perform date arithmetic on them:

    ```python
    from datetime import datetime, timedelta
    import pytz
    from dateutil import tz

    TORd = tz.gettz('America/Toronto')
    TORp = pytz.timezone('America/Toronto')
    HOUR = timedelta(hours=1)

    t0_u = datetime(2011, 11, 6, 5, 30, tzinfo=tz.tzutc())  # Folded date

    t0_d = t0_u.astimezone(TORd)
    t0_p = t0_u.astimezone(TORp)

    print("PYTZ during fold: {} (Should be EDT)".format(t0_p.tzname()))
    print("dateutil during fold: {} (Should be EDT)".format(t0_d.tzname()))

    print("PYTZ after fold: {} (Should be EST)".format((t0_p + HOUR).tzname()))
    print("dateutil after fold: {} (Should be EST)".format((t0_d + HOUR).tzname()))
    ```

    Result:

    ```
    PYTZ during fold: EDT (Should be EDT)
    dateutil during fold: EDT (Should be EDT)
    PYTZ after fold: EDT (Should be EST)
    dateutil after fold: EST (Should be EST)
    ```

I think the correct way to do things is to have the `fold` quality be an aspect of the datetime object itself. That said, I'm not sure how folding is handled with date arithmetic in gaps. For example, I would expect that if you add `timedelta(hours=1)` to `datetime(2011, 6, 4, 30, tzinfo=tz.gettz('America/Toronto'))`, you should be able to automatically get back `datetime(2011, 6, 1, 30, tzinfo=tz.gettz('America/Toronto', fold=1)`. Haven't taken a close enough look at the PEP495 PR to see if that's the case, but it's definitely not the case here (since there are no hooks in to `tzinfo` during date arithmetic operations as far as I know).

A couple points:

`writable` is only a property on `Dirent` objects in CWL 1.0, and not anywhere else

But the spec says that when the `InitialWorkDirRequirement` `listing` is an Expression it can only return `File`s and/or `Directory`s, not `Dirent`s.

However, `cwltool` does accept and process correctly an Expression for `listing` that returns an array containing a `Dirent`

But playing with this beyond-spec behavior I am not seeing a way to specify `writable: true` recursively for an entire directory. You can't put a `Dirent` object inside a synthetic `Directory` object, for example.

bonus: even with `writable: false`, `cwltool` doesn't make directories read-only, one can delete files from them

To summarize: We have a clear user story for marking an entire Directory tree as writable. One way forward is to issue a clarification that `writable: true` for a Directory is recursive and then to update the implementations to match.

The `marshmallow.validator` decorator is implemented in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/591729993b8e2b9e12316b6889b86b3b6206354b. 

See the "Schema-level validation" section of the docs for example usage: https://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/dev/extending.html#schema-level-validation. 

The actual API has a minor difference from @taion 's proposal above: there is no need to pass the field name to the `validator` decorator. This is because you can specify field names in the `ValidationError` constructor:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields, validator, ValidationError

class MySchema(Schema):
    bob = fields.Field()

    @validator
    def validate_bob(self, data):
        #...
        raise ValidationError('bob is invalid', 'bob')
```

The rationale for specifying field names on ValidationError is discussed in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/47.

Now that we have method decorators for preprocessors, postprocessors, and validators, I'm closing the issue for now.
How would this behave differently from the `only` parameter?

> Dot notation could hypothetically be used with ValidationErrors as well.

Sure. Decorators are just sugar. There will always be another way to specify the same validator, but in several circumstances it will be a much less elegant way. We could call it `@elegant_sugar`!

> Validators can made reusable by using callable objects:

If you use a decorated validator it's because you DON'T want to make it reusable. It's mainly because it makes sense only in a particular schema, for a particular use case. In my example what you really make reusable is the field, not the validator.

> Therefore, if the above proposal does get implemented, it would probably be as a separate decorator.

Ok. My preference goes to `@validates`.

---

Upon further reflection, I think we can do even better and, at the same time, solve the "error storage" problem.

```python
class Person(Schema):
    age = fields.Integer()
    
    @validates('age')
    def ensure_adult(self, value):
        if value < 18:
            return 'Too young!'
```

The `@validates` decorator raises a ValidationError('Too young!', 'age') only if the decorated method returns a string. If `None` is returned no exception will be raised.

Ok, now I see more clearly what you are doing: "absolute error" means "absolute value of the relative error".  This gets you in trouble when the zenith is at or near zero, which happens at latitudes < 23.  The other trouble when the zenith is very small is that a small change in sun position along the path of the sun creates a large change in azimuth angle.

I think you should be looking at degree differences rather than percentage difference, and even that won't fix the latter problem.  I also suggest running this at minute rather than hourly intervals and/or pick fixed intervals of solar time and I think you will lose some of the funny stuff.

@wholmgren and @cwhanse Thanks for the additional background, and again thanks for you patience as I come up to speed on PV systems and "out of the weeds" of single diode modeling and calibration. I will do my best to read up on those issues as quickly as I can find time for. My reaction so far to `ModelChain` w.r.t. `PVSystem` is that I think certain functionality would be better delegated to `PVSystem` (and merely orchestrated by `ModelChain` for integrated performance simulations). For example, the scaling of the DC power based on the _system_ configuration seems to belong in `PVSystem`.

Echoing Cliff's statement about one important goal of PVLib, I think we really should strive to make `ModelChain` bring clarity and "orchestration" to the high level modeling steps/process, and banish the implementation details to classes like `PVSystem` and `Location`. Right now I don't think that this is sufficiently well done in `ModelChain`. For example, I now have some code where `PVSystem` defines a `model_dc` property that is assigned a callable (e.g., a function, including user-defined) that is exposed to `ModelChain` via the method `calc_model_dc`:

```python
class PVSystem(object):
...
    def calc_model_dc(self, irrad, temp):
        """
        Calculate the DC model.

        Use the model specified in model_dc, which may be a user-defined
        function (more precisely, any callable obeying the API shown here).

        TODO Details, inc. irrad and temp being "generic"
        """

        return self.scale_voltage_current_power(
            self.model_dc(irrad, temp, **self.module_parameters))
...
```

Also, I have struggled a bit to accept the seemingly ["non-DRY"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don%27t_repeat_yourself) wrappers of low level functions with `PVSystem` methods, but now I think I can see the flexibility that this affords some users' workflows. I think making consistent DC API's among pvwatts, sapm, and sdm (i.e., all functions that can be assigned to the `PVSystem.model_dc` property) will do a lot to lower the "development pain" here.

I should have some new code implementing these ideas coming soon in #478.

Properties of the ELB have been split up in multiple components for the ALB, and some were added.

* [LoadBalancer](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-elasticloadbalancingv2-loadbalancer.html): Basically `scheme`, `securityGroup`, `subnet`.
* [Listener](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-elasticloadbalancingv2-listener.html): `port`, `protocol`, `certificates`
* [ListenerRule](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-elasticloadbalancingv2-listenerrule.html): Use this to forward requests to target groups based on the request path
* [TargetGroup](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-elasticloadbalancingv2-targetgroup.html): Describes a set of EC2s along with health check config, VPC and such.

In case anyone else is on the same boat and is trying to get `z3` on travis, here's my experience:

I added the following to my `.travis.yaml` file to get the ubuntu version downloaded/unpacked:

```
before_install
  - export Z3=`wget -q -O- http://github.com/Z3Prover/bin/raw/master/nightly | grep 'z3.*x64.*ubuntu.*.zip<' | awk '{print $7}' | awk -F= '{print $2}' | awk -F\" '{print $2}'`
  - export Z3Base=`basename $Z3 .zip`
  - wget --quiet -L -O z3.zip http://github.com/Z3Prover/bin/raw/master/nightly/$Z3
  - unzip -q z3.zip -d z3_downloaded
  - /bin/rm z3.zip
  - export PATH=$PATH:$PWD/z3_downloaded/$Z3Base/bin
  - z3 --version
```

This was quite tricky to get right: Note how there's a sequence of `awk`/`grep` tricks to precisely find the version to download, since your releases have hash-numbers on them and `wget` is kind of finicky about wildcards. To solve that issue, I did two calls to `wget`: One to get the directory listing, and second to find out the exact name of the `.zip` file I want. I wonder if there's an easier and more robust way to do this.

When I did that, it would unpack `z3`, put it in the path etc., correctly; but it actually would give me an error in the last line when I actually ran it:

     $ z3 --version
     z3: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6: version `GLIBCXX_3.4.18' not found (required by z3)

After googling around a bit, I found that the ubuntu images for regular travis is rather old by now. But there's an easy fix. I put the following at the top of my `.travis.yaml` file:

```
dist: trusty
```
It appears `trusty` is Travis's new image, which is modernized and comes with other bells and whistles. I cannot claim I understand all the implications, but the above did the trick for me.

_**A suggestion:**_ The above seems to work; though a bit clunky. It would be awesome if you can create a nightly release with the following naming schema:

```    
     z3-nightly-latest-ubuntu-x64.zip
```

And you can imagine a similar one for other OS's and architectures. That way there would be no guess work nor any fancy `awk`-`grep` lines to grab the latest, without having to figure out what the hash-code is. This is by no means essential, but could be nice for future deployments.

# Where to put setup.py

We can have the `setup.py` outside of the root, but this complicates things. It makes one of two problems:

1. If we have it in a subdirectory, it becomes impossible to just `pip install git+https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3`. This is annoying -- you won't be able to install the "cutting edge" git version very easily.
2. If we want to be able to install the git version easily, we could use a git subtree with an external git repo just for setup.py. That's *also* annoying, because another repo is involved.

Personally, I would prefer option 3 -- have setup.py in the root directory. It's the standard way to go, and it's analogous to having the `debian

Hi Christoph, 
thanks for the bug fix and the hint to the SMTLIB standard!

Perhaps I am missing some important point here, but I find the encoding proposed in the standard very cumbersome from a practical point of view. Having to use an assertion each time that one intends to map a floating point term to a bit-vector complicates the construction of terms very much. In fact, it breaks our entire architecture. Am I correct that one cannot build a term that involves such a conversion alone and that one has to interact with the solver to introduce "helper variables" such as b accompanied by an additional assertion for each conversion from FP to BV?

The only alternative that I see is to axiomatize the to_ieee_bv operation using an uninterpreted function symbol. But this obviously can't be the solution for a "simple" conversion between theories either (actually, Z3 reports unknown already when checking the example below). 
```
(declare-fun convert_to_ieee_bv ((_ FloatingPoint 8 24)) (_ BitVec 32))
(assert (forall ((x (_ FloatingPoint 8 24))) (= x ((_ to_fp 8 24) (convert_to_ieee_bv x)))))
```
Therefore I am very much in favor of having an operator such as to_ieee_bv for converting from floating-point numbers to bit vectors. For the case of a conversion of NaN into a bit vector, would it be possible to leave the significand bits and the sign bit unspecified but to enforce a 1-bit vector for the exponent? Doesn't this encode the equivalence class for NaN correctly? 

Currently, the operator fp.to_ieee_bv as implemented in Z3 yields the bit-vector term 0 if applied to NaN. Is this intended? The code snippet below reproduces the behavior in Z3:
```ocaml
let () =
  let ctx = Z3.mk_context [] in
  let float_nan = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_nan ctx (Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_sort_32 ctx) in
  let ieee_bv = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_to_ieee_bv ctx float_nan in
  Printf.printf "mk_to_ieee_bv(%s) = %s; after simplify: %s\n"
    (Z3.Expr.to_string float_nan)
    (Z3.Expr.to_string ieee_bv)
    (Z3.Expr.to_string (Z3.Expr.simplify ieee_bv None))
```
I am aware that the SMTLIB standard states that fp.to* are unspecified for NaN; but I cannot see the reason for that and simplifying to a zero bit vector as Z3 currently does, seems to be wrong anyways.

As a side node: The MathSAT5 API offers an operator similar to Z3's to_ieee_bv (see the function msat_make_fp_to_bv here http://mathsat.fbk.eu/apireference.html#a26a0d2d2ead14bb1f538ccd4f24517f4).

> Would setting `b=T.zeros` ensure that the bias is not a "trainable" parameter?

Sort of. It will call `T.zeros(shape)` to initialize the bias. It will still have the "trainable" flag in `layer.params[layer.b]`, but by default, `lasagne.layers.get_all_params()` only returns the shared variables that participate in the Theano expressions making up the network parameters. Hrm. I don't want to penalise successful lookups too much: they'll happen a lot. If our memory overhead is static (that is, if we don't allocate a new collection of data structures for each encoder/decoder) then I'm happy to accept the overhead.

Here are my findings based on these static header table implementations (lower is always better):
Tested on Python 3.5.2 w/ 5,000,000 lookups for each test

Size of the static table: 7,496 bytes

### Tuple Dict Method

Structure size: 9,024 bytes
Successful Name, Value Lookup: 1.717s
Partial Name, Value Lookup: 2.569s
Missed Name, Value Lookup: 2.386s

### Proposed Method

Structure size: 31,312 bytes
Successful Name, Value Lookup: 2.007s
Partial Name, Value Lookup: 2.198s
Missed Name, Value Lookup: 1.402s

From this data I'd conclude that if memory problems aren't our concern then I would recommend the proposed method in this pull request due to the much lower partial/miss lookup times.

@olupton I think what we talk about here is the difference between the packing up of the release area (what ```prepare``` does) and the specification of the job (which options to give to the gaudirun.py) script. It would not harm the jobs above if ```job1.py``` and ```job2.py``` were in the same shared area as long as I could request that job 1 is using the first options file and job2 the second.

```
j=Job()
myApp1 = GaudiExec()
myApp1.directory = "$SOMEPATH/DaVinciDev_v40r2"
myApp1.options = ["$SOMEPATH/DaVinciDev_v40r2/myDaVinciOpts_1.py"]
myApp1.prepare()

myApp2 = myApp1.copy()

# You would like to be able to do this without losing the prepared area
# through an unprepare call.
myApp1.options = ["$SOMEPATH/DaVinciDev_v40r2/myDaVinciOpts_2.py"]
```

I think this could be achieved through exploring the difference that we already have between ```ProtectedAttributeError``` and ```ReadOnlyObjectError```. So in this case ```myApp1.directory``` would throw an ```ProtectedAttributeError``` after the prepare state, but ```myApp1.options``` would be allowed to be changed. After submission, both would throw ```ReadOnlyObjectError```.

> In my opinion two GangaObjects should only be equal if all their schema attributes are the same

:+1:

I totally agree with this. This is I think the _expected_ behaviour of a comparison between GangaObjects.

There is room for discussion however about what **all schema attributes** means then. i.e.  do we want to ignore `id` and have objects with similar non `comparable` attributes compare equal. Or do we want it to essentially refer to **all** schema attributes such that the above fails as matt says.

The third option of course is to only have them equal if they are indeed the same object in memory which would in a practical sense look the same as the latter from above as the id would be expected to be different (however it could be forced to be the same).

 I flip flop between which I consider the most _expected_ behaviour from the users point of view. I suspect also there may be a dependence on the particular GangaObject in question as to what the _expected_ behaviour would be. With Jobs for example I think that people would not expect two Jobs with identical parameters (except the id) to compare equal as there is a sense that they are distinct jobs. However my feeling is that for a GangaList, the reverse is probably true.

> If we really want this latter comparison then an is_similar_to() function might make sense.

I don't think we want this.

The following snippet should be able to take images
```
import mujoco_py
import numpy as np
from scipy.misc import imsave
import time

fullpath = './example.xml'
model = mujoco_py.MjModel(fullpath)
width = 1000
height = 1000
viewer = mujoco_py.MjViewer(visible=True, init_width=width, init_height=height)
viewer.start()
viewer.set_model(model)

steps = 100
skip = 10

start = time.time()
for i in xrange(steps):
    viewer.render()
    viewer.loop_once()
    data, width, height = viewer.get_image()
    img = np.fromstring(data, dtype='uint8').reshape(height, width, 3)[::-1,:,:]
    imsave('imgs/out_' + str(i) + '.png', img)
    for j in xrange(skip):
        model.step()
end = time.time()
print(end - start)

viewer.finish()
viewer = None
```

from constance import LazyConfig
