```
The beacon request command is used by a device to locate all coordinators within its radio communications range during an active scan.
```
and
```
coordinator: A device in an low rate wireless personal area network (LR WPAN) that provides synchronization services to other devices in the LR WPAN.
full-function device: A device capable of operating as a coordinator.
reduced-function device (RFD): A device that is not capable of operating as a coordinator.
```
Thread device types are very similar to these. I, personally, map them like this:
Border-routers and routers - FFD, acting as coordinators.
REED - FFD, not acting as coordinator.
SED - RFD.

Combining the definitions with the mapping, gives that only [Border] Routers should respond to beacon requests. If I'm not mistaken, REED devices in my setup do respond with beacons (can't check that ATM). However the real question is what should OpenThread do when only 802.15.4 layer is active (i.e. Thread is disabled). Could not find any specifics on this, so it may be undefined.

The way OpenThread implementation currently works and what the 802.15.4 standard says, I would conclude that each device, right from the startup (interface up) creates it's own WPAN.
IMO before Thread layer is started, the 802.15.4 layer should act as RFD. There could be otInterfaceEnableCoordinator(bool) command. By default it would be false and only when Thread device becomes a router it would be set to tru. So [border] routers would respond to beacon requests and PCAP, joiner, REED and SED "modes" - would not.

@nibanks I think the next step is to walk through all the feedback from @pvanhorn and @darconeous and make sure you are addressing those comments. An quick attempt to summarize follows.  I'm using the terminology "NCP" build for the case of OT running on the radio device, and "Transceiver" build for the case of OT running on a host device.  

1) Make sure the solution is complete, working, and satisfies the desired use case before calling for merge (include a working example of the Transceiver configuration).
2) Plan for and fully account for the harder cases, specifically frame pending handling of a parent with many children.  Another case to consider is how do inbound and outbound messages queue on the Transceiver?  Perhaps look at the SPI API of some common 15.4 Transceivers such as CC2420 or RF233 for inspiration.
3) Allow developer to compile-time configure whether NCP or Transceiver mode is used.  A dedicated Transceiver-only configuration should support targeting very small RAM/flash parts.
4) Seek to define Transceiver radio API that meets requirements of that configuration as an independent superset of the stable NCP radio API (addition of the extra Getter functions, but also and any additions for frame pending, message queuing, etc).  An NCP configuration should not be forced to implement all the Transceiver driver API additions.
5) Reconsider the dual-mode use case (both NCP and Transceiver capable firmware).  Is dedicated firmware okay? If not, do both modes need to run in parallel?  If the later is required (parallel dual mode where OT runs on NCP yet Host can inject Transceiver packets), function pointers won't work per @darconeous.

A rough diagram of the "dedicated Transceiver firmware" design follows:
```
   Host                                      Transceiver FW
============
 OpenThread
============
 otPlatRadio
============                                 ==============
 Transceiver config
 posix driver
 implementation     <--------------->        (Spinel PHY subset)
 (Spine PHY Subset)
============                                 ==============
                                              spinelPlatRadio
                                             ==============
                                              otPlatRadio 
                                             + Transceiver extensions
                                             ==============
                                             platform/radio implementation
                                             ==============
```

Such ASCII art diagrams aren't the best...  It may be easier to build consensus via a brief design proposal or specification in a collaborative tool such as Google Docs or the wiki.

There are multiple problematic results here, which may have a similar fix.

When `pyexcel` is loading a dict, it replaces empty cells with `""`.

Empty cells in CVS files loaded with `pyexcel-io` are removed , due to https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel-io/commit/00973b4da9 , and then `pyexcel` replaces the missing with `""`.

So the `pyexcel-text` column formatting problem is because the column contains numbers and empty strings, so it renders the column as strings.

Issues when loading a dict isnt a serious problem -- the caller can fix the data.  However when loading a csv file, using `pyexcel-io`, the caller shouldnt be expected to know that they need to restore the missing cells.

So the serious problem here is that `-text` is not compatible with `-io`'s csv loader.

One possible workaround is for `pyexcel-text` to replace `""` cells with None, and see if tabulate ignores the None when determining the data type for the column.

I’m setting up the convert functions like this:

    from pmagpy import convert_2_magic as convert
    convert._2g_bin(args)

I think that syntax should be pretty readable, @ltauxe let me know if you have a different preference.

I’m making sure we have tests for all of the import scripts that don’t have them, and I’m also rewriting the scripts so that they use default arguments instead of `kwargs.get()` calls.  In other words, a lot of them currently look like this:

    def _2g_bin(**kwargs):
	 dir_path = kwargs.get(“dir_path”, “.”)
         ….

but will be updated to:

    def _2g_bin(dir_path=“.”, …)
        …


I think this is cleaner because it means people won’t be able to pass in invalid or misspelled arguments, and then get confused as to why defaults are being used instead.  With the first function, convert._2g_bin(dirpath=‘/path/to/directory/‘) would *work*, but the invalid argument “dirpath" would be ignored, potentially to the user's great confusion.  With the second function, convert._2g_bin(dirpath=‘/path/to/directory/‘) would raise an error:

    TypeError: _2g_bin() got an unexpected keyword argument 'dirpath'

Otherwise, the convert function will work the same way as before.  You can still pass in a dict with the arguments:

    args = {‘dir_path’: ‘/path/to/directory’}
    convert._2g_bin(**args)

or

    convert._2g_bin(dir_path=“.”)


To sum up, all of the import scripts are converted to MagIC 3, but they still need to be reworked into the convert_2_magic.py module, and tests need to be added.  

Lastly, the questions about mst_magic, microwave_magic, and old_jr6_magic still need to be resolved (see above).

I think a lot of the comment in question are really internal notes to developers. For those, the format matters less and could even be in the body of the function. For comments that document public API, the format should be more rigid.

> alright, I'm still hesitant to joint the buffer_info is not copyable club

Well, you *could* implement a copy by copying everything except `ownview`, setting `ownview` to false: the original would keep ownership, the copy wouldn't.  This has a major problem though: if I make a copy, then let the original be destroyed, `view` is now pointing to freed memory.  So a copy wouldn't be so much copying as referencing the original.  That doesn't seem ideal.  You could maybe work around this by copying the `ownview` value, then using a `shared_ptr` with a custom deleter that doesn't actually do the deletion if the first object was initialized from an externally-owned pointer—but that's getting messy.

And now that I think about it, the same lifetime problem is in the current version with `ownview = false`: `view` is only valid as long as the actual owner is still alive, but we have no way to know whether that is the case (aside from just being careful).  Perhaps there's a better way around the whole thing, but I'm not really sure what that would be.

---
> I'm still green on rvalues/moves so I put them there for extra explicitness. I kind of think its a good reminder too, of what's happening.

A bit off-topic, but a simple rule of thumb: any value that is "in transit," i.e. that won't exist after the current expression, is automatically an rvalue.  The danger with throwing an `std::move` around something is that the whole point of `std::move` is to convert lvalue references into rvalue references.  Sometimes you explicitly want to do that, usually when you don't intend to use a local variable again, and that's exactly what `std::move` is about: "I know this value is an lvalue, but I'm not going to use it again, so do what you want with it."  You won't break anything putting it around something that is already an rvalue, but you may well if you unintentionally put it around an lvalue.

Re: multiple inheritance

It might be feasible, and I renamed `try_implicit_conversion` to `loaded_or_alternatives` with this in mind.  I'm not entirely sure if overloading implicitly_convertible() for this is the right way to go, however: base class use is simply a cast, not a conversion, but that's a minor detail.  Whatever the user-facing interface, loaded_or_alternatives() would be the place for it: if regular loading fails, this could then try base class casting for known base classes, then if that fails, implicit conversion (or implicit conversion could be tried first; it probably makes no difference).

One comment regarding:
> When a method G::f() is called with an A instance, my hope was that the recursive implicit conversion code would cause these conversions to take place by running the conversion from A to C and then from C to G (it would be no problem if that happens in a sort of brute force manner, which tries out some invalid conversions as well)
We really don't want to (and I think, cannot) use the implicit conversion mechanism for this for at least a couple reasons:
1. If `G::f()` is called with `this` set to a pointer to a new (temporary) `G` instance, i.e. via implicit conversion, things are not going to work as expected because the G instance *isn't* the base instance of the caller, it's a copy of the caller's `G` superclass instance.
2. `G` might not be implicitly convertible at all, e.g. if it's an abstract base class that *has* `f()` but only has `virtual void f2() = 0;`
3. Even if `G` is not abstract, overridden virtual methods would break: `void f() { g(); }` where g() is virtual but overridden by `C` or `A` won't do the right thing.

But still, this seems quite feasibly by using a similar implementation, but by doing a static_cast<Base*>() pointer cast instead of an implicit conversion.

I'd like to see Seth's comments on this since he's the one that purposed `--reuse-key`.

With that said, I think there is a use case where `--key-path` is significantly more cumbersome than `--reuse-key`. If you want Certbot to generate your key for you on your first run and then reuse the same key thereafter, you'll either manually have to modify the renewal configuration file (which we currently don't encourage or really tell people how to do) to add `key_path` or renew your cert once manually using `certonly`/`run` including the `--key-path` flag so it's added to your configuration file for you. It's certainly doable, but to me it feels a little harder than it should be.

For my personal opinion, pretending for a minute this PR doesn't exist, I think I'd prefer to initially only support `--key-path` and see if something like `--reuse-key` is a commonly requested feature. Since this feature has already been implemented though at the request of a Certbot developer and it improves the UX in some cases, I'm inclined to take this PR with `--reuse-key`.

There are a few reasons why we wouldn't default to certonly. In this case it's because certonly would invoke --standalone, which would spin up a web server on your computer. This would try to bind to port 443. But we've already detected that you have a webserver running, which would prevent certbot from binding to 443. Which means that it'd likely fail. Alternately we could use the webroot authenticator, but again we'd need to know that your webserver is configured to serve files in that way, in which case I think we'd want to know that you've opted into that.

Okey so I did it on an 1.6GB file. Here is the code:

```python
import hashlib
from xxhash import xxh64

def fileMD5(filename):
    '''Calculate partial MD5, basically the first and last 8M
    of the file for large files. This should signicicantly reduce
    the time spent on the creation and comparison of file signature
    when dealing with large bioinformat ics datasets. '''
    # calculate md5 for specified file
    md5 = hashlib.md5()
    block_size = 2**20  # buffer of 1M
    try:
        # 2**24 = 16M
            with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
                while True:
                    data = f.read(block_size)
                    if not data:
                        break
                    md5.update(data)
    except IOError as e:
        sys.exit(f'Failed to read {filename}: {e}')
    return md5.hexdigest()

def fileH64(filename):
    md5 = xxh64()
    block_size = 2**20  # buffer of 1M
    try:
        # 2**24 = 16M
            with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
                while True:
                    data = f.read(block_size)
                    if not data:
                        break
                    md5.update(data)
    except IOError as e:
        sys.exit(f'Failed to read {filename}: {e}')
    return md5.hexdigest()
```

You can support passing a raw_password to factory_boy like so:

```class UserFactory(Factory):
    password = factory.PostGeneration(lambda user, create, extracted: user.set_password(extracted))```

The `create` arg is irrelevant here, that simply specifies whether you're using the build or create strategy – `set_password` doesn't `save` so it doesn't matter which

The `extracted` arg is what was passed to the factory call – by default this is `None` so you end up with an unusable password – which is probably fine – if you pass something it sets it using the method as per your 

```In [1]: UserFactory().has_usable_password()
Out[1]: False

In [2]: UserFactory(password='test').has_usable_password()
Out[2]: True

In [3]: UserFactory(password='test').check_password('test')
Out[3]: True

In [4]: UserFactory(password='test').password
Out[4]: 'argon2$argon2i$v=19$m=512,t=2,p=2$blahblahblah'
```

---


To clarify, the reason you have to set the password in `PostGeneration` is because up until that point (e.g. when `LazyAttribute`'s are evaluated – factory_boy is working with a user stub – not a user object – so the `set_password` method is not available – the stub is later used to build the model instance (because you might not be building a model instance, factory_boy support building dicts and other things too)

> Does this include both the hardware controlled arraylike parameters (which you don't set but could be described as having a multi-valued return) and for example b field parameter with x, y and z values?

Yes. Anything whose values are more complicated than a single number (or string).

We may decide that's an oversimplification - which is part of why I posted this now rather than waiting until I finish the example notebooks. Basically, what we had before was this horrible `Parameter` constructor with [5 cases to choose from](https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument/parameter.py#L78-L86) and different behavior of the arguments in each case. I realized that in terms of functionality we could reduce it to 2 cases (the old case 1 and case 5), and break them into separate classes, because the simple case makes it easy to make simple parameters, and the most complicated case encompasses all the others.

So far the only real limitation I see that this imposes is that if you want to return a single array (`[1, 2, 3]` or numpy equivalent), you need to wrap it in an extra layer (`[[1, 2, 3]]`) because it's specified in a form that *can* return multiple arrays. I suppose that could be confusing if you want to manually look at the result of `param.get()`... If folks think that's too cumbersome, I could certainly put back case 3, either as an optional feature of `Parameter` (specify `shape`, and the setpoint info) or as a separate class like `ArrayParameter`.
In that case I'll reopen as a reminder and we can close when it's done.

One thing I would add in addition to that is a check in `give_to_local_scheduler_retry` to see if the current local scheduler assignment is dead. If yes, then cache the task and don't try again. Else, retry the request. This will help get rid of all the warning messages that we see right now if a local scheduler dies, and other local schedulers just keep continually.

Also, be careful with reconstruction suppression. We only want to submit the actor creation task once. Ideally, we would reconstruct the result of the initial creation task, instead of directly resubmitting it, so we can reuse the current suppression mechanism.

The develop branch altered the way configurations are done: see http://datacube-core.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/config.html

This is one way to do it:

example `.datacube.conf`
```
[user]
#default_environment: datacube
default_environment: s3aio_env

[datacube]
db_hostname: <host_name>
db_database: <db_name>
db_username: <db_username>

[s3aio_env]
db_hostname: <host_name>
db_database: <db_name>
db_username: <db_username>

index_driver: s3aio_index
```

You can use the `default_environment` to set the default environment you wish to use `datacube` or `s3aio_env` in this example.

An alternative to `default_environment` is to initialise Datacube with one of the environments in `.datacube.conf`
```dc = Datacube(env='s3aio_env')```

And right, we don't want to get rid of `real_socket_type` entirely, because we still need to work on 3.5 and 3.6. Instead, the idea would be to make Trio's socket object – on all versions – act like Python's socket object acts in 3.7+. And conveniently, Python's socket object in 3.7+ sets its `.type` attribute to be the same thing that Trio is already calculating with its `real_socket_type` hack.

So basically this means defining the `.type` attribute on *trio* socket objects to be something like `real_socket_type(self._sock.type)`.

And then we could go through all the places in trio that currently use `real_socket_type` and switch them to using `trio_socket.type` (it looks like there are currently 3 places, and they all have a trio socket object available).

Does that make sense?

I wrote some deduplication code for a work-related project that works for S1 images and expects a DataFrame as input. This could be extended to support S2 and S3 as well and perhaps work on dicts instad of dataframes. It uses the acquisition time instead of simply relying on product names since SciHub occasionally (for about 1% of images) hosts identical images under different names as well.

```python
def _find_duplicates():
    df = pd.read_sql_table(S1Image.__tablename__, Session.connection()).set_index("uuid")

    # Sentinel-1 image slices are 25-seconds long for IW and 16 seconds for SM.
    # We assume that images that are closer than 15 seconds are duplicates.
    THR_SECONDS = 15

    def find_identical_images(rows):
        duplicates = []
        group = []
        for idx, t in rows["beginposition"].sort_values().iteritems():
            if group and (t - group[0][1]).seconds < THR_SECONDS:
                group.append((idx, t))
            else:
                if len(group) > 1:
                    duplicates.append([x[0] for x in group])
                group = [(idx, t)]
        return duplicates

    # Find all groups of duplicate images
    groups = []
    for idx, group in df.groupby(["platformname", "platformnumber", "producttype", "resolution"]):
        groups += find_identical_images(group)

    # Remove the most recently ingested image and flatten the list
    duplicates = []
    for g in groups:
        duplicates += list(df.loc[g].sort_values("ingestiondate", ascending=False)
                           .index.tolist())[1:]
    return duplicates
```

Hi @linkerzhang.

>  In this PR, I removed the attribute reference, however, I just realize that it should be added back since it's needed even for 1st motivation. We need to take function similar as operator definition, which contains attribute declaration but no data.

Yeah, this is a good reason to not take on too many motivations, because you might discover that you shouldn't actually solve them via the same mechanism.

Remember that for motivation 1 (defining composite operators in the ONNX spec), the actual function definitions never occur in model files; they occur in the ONNX operator database. So there is no reason to *necessarily* force user-defined functions and composite operator functions to coincide, and indeed, in the way that ONNX is setup right now, if you want this particular technical proposal (add a FunctionDef accessible from ModelDef) to work for motivation 1, there's a lot of other work you have to do (how are you getting the composite operator defs to the consumers? What if a consumer is operating on an older version of the ONNX library?)

Let me offer a counter-proposal, which was communicated to me from @dzhulgakov. For motivation 1, we should defer the question of designing a protobuf format for backends (where composite operator definitions would live.) Instead, we should treat this as a source transformation problem: an operator unfolding is defined via a *program* which translates from a composite operator into its constituent elements. There are a few good properties of this scheme:

1. You side-step the problem of how to get operator definitions to backends. Instead, the onus is on the *user* to run the program to desugar the operators, and then pass it on to the backends. This decoupling means that we can easily update the desugarer independently of the backend (which might be something like Windows OS and cannot really get upgraded easily.)

2. You side-step the problem of defining an expressive enough language to represent function operators. As I mentioned in our call, once you have attribute references, you'll have people wanting to compute on the attribute references, and anything you do you'll have to implement in your interpreter.

The only problem is that this doesn't let you solve motivation 2 (indicating shared subgraphs upon export), but that's why we are still going to take this technical proposal (adding FunctionDef to model proto), but without attribute references. And since I know solving motivation 2 is a priority for MS, I'd suggest deferring motivation 1 for now.

It will be helpful for people reading the conversation if the wiki page gets updated.

P.S. I do want to bikeshed the details of the protobuf but let's get this part established first.

There isn't a specific CLI command to do that, but you could use python to query the WFS service using this http://sentinelhub-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ogc.html#sentinelhub.ogc.WebFeatureService

Or HTTP get (e.g. with CURL) the data using a WFS request directly, as demonstrated in this faq (https://www.sentinel-hub.com/faq#t29n349), e.g.:

https://services.sentinel-hub.com/ogc/wfs/<CONFIGURATION_INSTANCE_ID>?service=WFS&version=2.0.0&request=GetFeature&time=2015-01-01/2017-04-20/P1D&typenames=DSS2&maxfeatures=100&srsname=EPSG:3857&bbox=-410925.4640611076,4891969.810251283,-391357.58482010243,4911537.689492286&outputformat=application/json

Note that you need a Sentinel Hub account to get the key (CONFIGURATION_INSTANCE_ID), and there needs to be a L2A layer added inside the instance, otherwise WFS service won't want to return data from the L2A collection.

> One shouldn't compute cosine for all-zero vectors, it's not defined.
I understand your point of view, but if you do happen to compute the cosine distance for an all-zero vector, Nmslib will currently (1) not inform the user of any issue and (2) silently return an incorrect distance.

I guess it comes down to who's responsible for validating user input and your point of view is it's the user.

> PS: if you produce NaNs it gets ugly one way or another later.
Actually, in our case it makes a lot of sense: if you filter the nearest neighbours on a max distance (e.g., dist < max_dist), that would fail for a NaN dist and so wouldn't return any candidates. With an incorrect dist of zero it looks like there are as many "perfect" nearest neighbour matches as there are vectors in the training set.
